D6 Display L4 Analyze Reference

ClearType on Real Glyphs

sample each letter three times across every pixel and the strokes sharpen — but the curves pick up red and cyan edges

ClearType rasterises text at triple horizontal resolution, then assigns each of the three samples to a pixel's red, green, and blue subpixel. Vertical stems gain effective sharpness; but on curves and diagonals the three channels disagree, so glyph edges show faint red and cyan fringes.

Compare greyscale and subpixel rendering of the same magnified glyphs. Greyscale is neutral but soft; subpixel is crisper, with the tell-tale coloured edges that only appear because the eye is meant to fuse them at normal size.
